attack_surface.exposure: Ed25519 key pair generation via NSec. Exported byte arrays on managed heap.
attack_surface.trust_boundary: Internal — only called by create handler and GetOrCreateDefaultAsync
gaps: Exported byte[] not zeroed after base64 encoding

attack_surface.exposure: Private key stored as plaintext string in SQLite (.db/system.sqlite)
IdentityVariable serialized through JsonStreamSerializer to channels
User-provided identity names used as DataSource keys
Auto-create on first access, sync-over-async in property getter
mitigations: Single source of truth in GetOrCreateDefaultAsync

attack_surface.exposure: Returns raw private key string in Data.Value

findings.vector: Exported Ed25519 byte arrays (privateKeyBytes) persist on managed heap after base64 encoding until GC collects them
findings.preconditions: Attacker has managed heap access (memory dump, debugger attach)
findings.impact: Private key material readable from process memory after key generation
findings.exploit_sketch: Attach debugger or dump process memory after identity creation. Scan for 32-byte Ed25519 seed patterns in heap.
findings.proposed_fix: Add CryptographicOperations.ZeroMemory(privateKeyBytes) after Convert.ToBase64String(). Defense-in-depth only — attacker with heap access already owns the process.

findings.vector: IdentityVariable.PrivateKey is a .NET string (immutable, not zeroable). Persists in memory until GC.
findings.impact: Private key readable from process memory for actor lifetime
findings.exploit_sketch: Same as finding 1. The string representation is even easier to find than raw bytes.
findings.proposed_fix: Architectural decision — SecureString is deprecated in .NET. Accept as inherent to managed runtime. The [Sensitive] attribute correctly prevents output-channel leakage.

findings.vector: Data.Envelope._envelopeJsonOptions does not include SensitivePropertyFilter. If IdentityVariable-bearing Data is compressed, private key is serialized unfiltered into the gzip payload.
findings.preconditions: Compress() must be called on Data containing IdentityVariable (currently zero call sites)
findings.impact: Private key leaks into compressed payload, which may cross transport boundaries
findings.exploit_sketch: Future: when Compress() is wired into channel output or Data transport, any Data wrapping an IdentityVariable would include the plaintext private key in the compressed JSON.
findings.proposed_fix: Add SensitivePropertyFilter.Filter to _envelopeJsonOptions TypeInfoResolver.Modifiers. Zero-risk change, defense-in-depth.

findings.vector: IdentityData caches the default IdentityVariable (including PrivateKey) for the actor's lifetime. Old values not zeroed on Update().
findings.impact: Previously-default identity's private key may persist in GC-unreachable but not-yet-collected heap memory
findings.exploit_sketch: Same as finding 1/2. Old IdentityVariable objects remain on heap after Update() replaces the cached reference.
findings.proposed_fix: Not actionable in managed runtime without SecureString. Accept as inherent.

summary: The identity module is well-designed for PLang's user-sovereign threat model. All output serialization paths correctly filter [Sensitive] properties. SQL injection is prevented by parameterized queries. Name validation is thorough. The only actionable finding is a theoretical gap in Data.Envelope.Compress() (medium, zero current call sites). All other findings are defense-in-depth suggestions rated low under the user-sovereign model. Verdict: PASS.
